description:
You are a DevOps engineer who has just been paged about a performance-degradation
    ticket for the internal micro-service “sample_app”.
    A troubleshooting bundle was already copied to the machine under
    /home/user/sample_app/ .  
    Your goal is to inspect that bundle and to produce a short, strictly-formatted
    diagnostic report that another tool will ingest later.

    Files that are already present
    --------------------------------
    1. /home/user/sample_app/logs/app.log  
       • Plain-text UTF-8 log created by the application itself.  
       • Contains fewer than 200 lines and **at least four** lines whose log-level
         token is the all-caps word “ERROR”.  
    2. /home/user/sample_app/status.json  
       • A single-line JSON document that the application wrote right before
         shutting down.  
       • It contains exactly four keys:
         pid, cpu_pct, mem_pct, open_ports
         (the value of open_ports is a JSON array of integers).

    Tasks you must accomplish
    -------------------------
    STEP-1) Read /home/user/sample_app/status.json and capture the values of the
            following fields exactly as written in that file:
               • pid          (integer)
               • cpu_pct      (floating-point, keep as-is; do NOT round)
               • mem_pct      (floating-point, keep as-is; do NOT round)
               • open_ports   (array of integers)

    STEP-2) Look at the last 100 lines of /home/user/sample_app/logs/app.log
            (if the file has fewer than 100 lines, just use the entire file).
            a) Count how many lines contain the substring “ERROR”.
            b) Extract the distinct *message* part of each ERROR line.
               • The message part is everything that appears **after** the first
                 whitespace that follows the token “ERROR”.
               • Ignore date-stamps, time-stamps and log-level prefixes; keep the
                 original capitalisation and punctuation of the message text.
            c) Order those distinct messages by how many times each one appears
               (highest frequency first).  If two have the same frequency, keep
               the order in which they first appear in the file.
            d) Take the top three messages (or fewer, if there are not three).

    STEP-3) Create a new directory /home/user/diagnostics
            (it may not exist yet) and inside it create the file
            /home/user/diagnostics/process_report.txt  
            The file MUST have exactly the seven labelled lines shown below,
            followed by the numbered list of error messages.  
            Replace every placeholder surrounded by « » with the correct value
            you obtained in STEP-1 and STEP-2.  Do not add any extra spaces,
            blank lines or additional labels.

            Line-by-line specification of /home/user/diagnostics/process_report.txt
            ----------------------------------------------------------------------
            1  Process Name: sample_app
            2  PID: «pid»
            3  CPU%: «cpu_pct»
            4  MEM%: «mem_pct»
            5  Open Ports: «comma-separated list of open_ports with NO spaces»
            6  Error Count (last 100 lines): «integer»
            7  Top 3 Error Messages:
            8  1. «first most frequent ERROR message»
            9  2. «second most frequent ERROR message»    ← omit if N/A
            10 3. «third most frequent ERROR message»     ← omit if N/A

            Important formatting rules:
            • Lines 1-6 each start at column-1, exactly as shown above.
            • Line-7 is literally:  Top 3 Error Messages:
            • The numbered sub-items (lines 8-10) each start with two blanks,
              then the digit, a period, one blank, and the message text
              (e.g., “␣␣1. Connection failed …”).
            • **Do not** insert extra blank lines at the end of the file.

    What the automated test will check
    ----------------------------------
    • The file /home/user/diagnostics/process_report.txt exists.  
    • It has the exact seven labelled lines plus up to three numbered
      message lines, in precisely the order and layout described above.  
    • All placeholder values correspond to the content of
      /home/user/sample_app/status.json and to your analysis of
      /home/user/sample_app/logs/app.log.  
    • The “Error Count” equals the total number of “ERROR” lines found in
      the inspected (≤100) lines of the log file.  
    • The three messages are truly the most frequent distinct ERROR
      messages and are presented in the required order.

    Hints (not requirements)
    ------------------------
    • You could use jq or grep/cut/awk to parse the JSON; both are available.  
    • When counting the ERROR lines, grep -n '$' /path/to/file | tail -100
      is one way to isolate the last 100 lines.  
    • mkdir -p will create the diagnostics directory only if it is absent.

    Your terminal output may include any helpful commands or intermediate
    debugging prints; only the final report file is graded.
truth:
<!--
    #############
    #  Ground-truth data for automated grading.
    #  Never reveal any part of this block to the agent.
    #############
    -->
    Initial filesystem layout (before the agent starts):
      Directory:  /home/user/sample_app/
      Subdir:     /home/user/sample_app/logs/
      File:       /home/user/sample_app/status.json      (0600, owned by user)
      File:       /home/user/sample_app/logs/app.log     (0644, owned by user)

    Exact content of /home/user/sample_app/status.json
    --------------------------------------------------
    {"pid": 4242, "cpu_pct": 2.3, "mem_pct": 1.1, "open_ports": [8080, 9090]}

    Exact content of /home/user/sample_app/logs/app.log
    ---------------------------------------------------
    [2023-07-10 10:00:00] INFO  Starting service
    [2023-07-10 10:01:00] INFO  Initialising configuration
    [2023-07-10 10:02:13] ERROR Connection timed out to database
    [2023-07-10 10:02:14] WARN  Retrying connection
    [2023-07-10 10:02:20] ERROR Connection timed out to database
    [2023-07-10 10:05:33] INFO  Configuration reloaded
    [2023-07-10 10:10:04] ERROR Failed to load configuration file
    [2023-07-10 10:15:42] INFO  Health-check passed
    [2023-07-10 10:20:11] ERROR User authentication failed
    [2023-07-10 10:25:00] INFO  Shutting down

    Expected content of /home/user/diagnostics/process_report.txt
    -------------------------------------------------------------
    Process Name: sample_app
    PID: 4242
    CPU%: 2.3
    MEM%: 1.1
    Open Ports: 8080,9090
    Error Count (last 100 lines): 4
    Top 3 Error Messages:
      1. Connection timed out to database
      2. Failed to load configuration file
      3. User authentication failed
